title: Arrays & Linked Lists
subject: Computer Science
objectives: Understand how arrays store data in memory
Compare arrays and linked lists for different operations

sections.heading: Arrays: contiguous memory
sections.content:
An array stores elements in consecutive memory locations. Think of it like a row of numbered mailboxes — each box is the same size and right next to the other.

Because of this structure:
• Accessing any element by index is instant: O(1). You can jump directly to mailbox #50.
• Inserting or deleting in the middle is slow: O(n). You might need to shift all subsequent elements to make room or fill the gap.

sections.heading: Linked lists: scattered nodes
sections.content:
A linked list stores elements as individual nodes, each containing data and a pointer to the next node. Think of a scavenger hunt where each clue tells you where to find the next one.

Because of this structure:
• Inserting or deleting at the beginning is instant: O(1). Just update one pointer.
• Accessing an element by position is slow: O(n). You must follow the chain from the start.
• Each node requires extra memory for the pointer.

sections.heading: When to use which
sections.content:
Use an array when:
• You need fast access by index (e.g., looking up student #42)
• The size of the collection is known and relatively fixed
• You need to iterate through all elements frequently

Use a linked list when:
• You frequently insert or delete elements at the beginning
• The size changes unpredictably
• You don't need random access by index

Example: A playlist where songs are frequently added/removed → linked list. High scores that are looked up by rank → array.

Arrays excel at random access (O(1)) but are slow for insertions/deletions. Linked lists excel at insertions/deletions (O(1) at the head) but are slow for random access. Choose based on your most common operations.

aiContext: This lesson covers arrays and linked lists at a high school computer science level. Students learn how each structure stores data, their time complexities for common operations, and when to choose one over the other.
